Q1: Honestly, I didn't have the most structured routine. I'd usually set aside blocks of time on weekends and a couple of evenings during the week, but I'm very visual so I'd spend a lot of that time creating diagrams and mind maps from the material rather than just reading through things. I found that about 21 hours a week worked for me, though it wasn't always spread evenly. I'd often batch my studying based on what felt most urgent or interesting at the time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was coming up. If I had exams approaching, I'd lock in and study more intensively, but during quieter weeks I might slack off a bit on the regular coursework. Deadlines drove a lot of my scheduling, and honestly, my extracurriculars sometimes meant I had to be flexible. I've learned to adapt quickly though, so irregular patterns don't stress me out much.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or wanting to check if there was new content posted. Sometimes my peers would message me about something in the discussion forum, and that would prompt me to log in. I wasn't one to just browse the LMS casually though—I went in with a purpose and got out. If there wasn't something specific pulling me in, I probably wouldn't bother logging in that day.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a daily habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, especially when assignments were due, but it felt more like a tool I used when necessary rather than something integrated into my routine. My real routine involved my own notes and external resources more than the platform itself.
Q5: The video lectures were genuinely helpful because I'm a visual learner and I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the content at my own pace. I watched a lot of video content—probably close to three hours worth. The discussion posts were also useful because connecting with peers helped me see different perspectives on the material. When I could visualize concepts or talk them through with others, that's when things really clicked for me.
Q6: I often skipped the reading-heavy resources or text-based supplementary materials. They just didn't stick for me the way visual content did, so I'd skim them at best. Some of the optional quizzes I ignored too because they felt repetitive after a while. I focused my energy on what I knew would actually help me learn and perform well, rather than trying to do everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped me understand a concept I was struggling with. I also valued anything that let me interact with peers because those conversations often clarified things better than studying alone. If something felt like busywork or didn't clearly contribute to my understanding or grade, I'd deprioritize it quickly.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like extras I could skip without consequences, which meant I often did skip them. Required activities had weight behind them, so I made sure to complete those even if I wasn't fully engaged. Optional stuff I'd only do if I was genuinely curious or if a peer recommended it. There's only so much time, and I had to be strategic about where I invested my effort.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded lecture slides and videos early on and studied from those offline constantly. I also made my own visual notes—color-coded diagrams, flowcharts, that kind of thing—which I'd review from my tablet or printed copies. I used YouTube videos and external websites too when the course material wasn't clicking. A lot of my real studying happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: Most of my exam prep wouldn't show up in the LMS at all. I'd spend hours working through my handwritten notes, redrawing diagrams until I had concepts memorized, and quizzing myself using flashcards I made. I also studied with friends in person sometimes, working through problems together. The LMS would just show I downloaded something once, but the actual learning happened offline over many sessions.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once and then work with it for weeks without ever going back to the LMS. My exam score reflects that this approach worked well for me. I preferred having materials saved locally so I could study without needing internet or dealing with the platform's interface. One download could mean hours of offline engagement.
Q12: There were stretches mid-semester where the content felt dry and I just got bored with the routine. When the material wasn't visually engaging or felt too theoretical, my motivation dipped. My extracurriculars also got busier at certain points, which split my attention. But I'm pretty resilient, so I'd push through those slumps without letting them derail me too much.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I had events or commitments outside of class, the LMS was the first thing I'd cut back on because I knew I could catch up later with my offline materials. Fatigue sometimes meant I'd log in but not really absorb anything, so I learned to study when I was actually alert rather than forcing it. Confusion occasionally made me avoid certain modules until I could discuss them with peers.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd scroll through content or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I'd technically be 'active' on the platform but not actually processing anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions. I learned to recognize when that was happening and would just step away instead of wasting time pretending to study.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Part of it was boredom—if something didn't grab my interest, I'd push it off. Part of it was knowing I could still pull through with strong exam performance, which I did. My resilience meant I could handle the crunch, but my assignment completion definitely suffered because of my tendency to delay.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my main driver for LMS activity. I'd ramp up my logins and engagement right before something was due, then drop off afterward. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but it worked for me. Without a deadline looming, I had little motivation to interact with the platform. The structure of due dates shaped almost all of my online behavior.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but not always immediately. If I got good feedback, it motivated me to keep going with my approach. Critical feedback made me reconsider and sometimes reach out to peers for support, which was helpful. But honestly, feedback often came too late to change how I approached the next task, so its impact was limited.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated but also maybe exploited a bit. Having so many online courses meant I could pick and choose where to focus my energy. The peer support intervention was actually really helpful—connecting with classmates kept me accountable and made the experience less isolating. But the structure also made it easy to disengage from the LMS when I wanted to.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for me. What made it harder was when modules were text-heavy or when the platform felt clunky to navigate. Also, having low-stakes activities that didn't clearly connect to assessments made it easy to skip them. Better integration of visual learning tools throughout would have kept me more consistently engaged.
Q20: I think those students might be exploring, trying to figure out what they need to focus on, or maybe they're anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually producing work. It could also be technical issues or personal circumstances that prevent them from completing submissions even though they're trying to engage. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That completely reflects my experience. My LMS engagement was pretty low, but I scored over 90 on my exam and got a Distinction. I studied effectively offline using methods that worked for my visual learning style. The LMS was just a delivery mechanism for me—the real learning happened elsewhere. Low platform activity doesn't mean low effort or poor understanding.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you log in, what you click on, and how long you spend on pages. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't capture whether I actually understood something, how much I studied offline, or the value of peer conversations that happened outside the platform. Logs show behavior, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material and putting in genuine effort to learn, not just going through the motions. For me, it's about being mentally present, making connections between concepts, and feeling some level of investment in my own progress. It's not about how many times I log in—it's about what's happening in my head when I study.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the hours I spent creating visual study materials, the peer study sessions I had in person, or how I used external resources to supplement my learning. It also wouldn't capture my resilience—how I bounced back from boredom or competing demands without getting derailed. My engagement was real and effective; it just happened mostly outside the platform's view.
